title: Employment Rights Act 1996
desc: The principal statute governing individual employment rights in Great Britain. Consolidates earlier employment protection legislation and covers employment particulars, protection of wages, time off rights, maternity rights, notice, unfair dismissal, and redundancy. Extensively amended since enactment including by ERA 2025.

sections.title: Statement of initial employment particulars
sections.summary: Employer must give employee a written statement of employment particulars not later than the beginning of employment.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employee begins employment with an employer, the employer shall give to the employee a written statement of particulars of employment not later than the beginning of the employment.

(2) The statement shall contain particulars of—
(a) the names of the employer and employee,
(b) the date when the employment began,
(c) the date on which the employee's period of continuous employment began (taking into account any employment with a previous employer which counts towards that period).

(3) The statement shall also contain a note specifying any disciplinary rules applicable to the employee or referring the employee to the provisions of a document specifying such rules which is reasonably accessible to the employee.

subs.text: The statement shall also contain particulars, as at a specified date not more than seven days before the statement is given, of the following: (a) the scale or rate of remuneration or the method of calculating remuneration; (b) the intervals at which remuneration is paid; (c) any terms and conditions relating to hours of work; (d) any terms and conditions relating to holiday entitlement; (e) the title of the job or a brief description of the work; (f) either the place of work or, where the employee is required or permitted to work at various places, an indication of that and of the address of the employer; (g) any collective agreements which directly affect the terms and conditions of the employment.

sections.title: Note about disciplinary procedures and pensions
sections.summary: The written statement must include a note about disciplinary rules, grievance procedures, and pensions.
sections.text:
(1) The statement required by section 1 shall include a note—
(a) specifying any disciplinary rules applicable to the employee, or referring the employee to the provisions of a document specifying such rules which is reasonably accessible to the employee,
(b) specifying, by description or otherwise—
(i) a person to whom the employee can apply if dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision relating to him, and
(ii) a person to whom the employee can apply for the purpose of seeking redress of any grievance relating to his employment, and the manner in which any such application should be made,
(c) where there are further steps consequent on any such application, explaining those steps or referring to the provisions of a document explaining them which is reasonably accessible to the employee.
refs: ACAS Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures 2015

sections.title: Statement of changes
sections.summary: Employer must give written statement of any change in particulars within one month of the change.
sections.text:
(1) If, after the material date, there is a change in any of the matters particulars of which are required by sections 1 to 3 to be included or referred to in a statement under section 1, the employer shall give to the employee a written statement containing particulars of the change.

(2) The statement under subsection (1) shall be given at the earliest opportunity and, in any event, not later than one month after the change.

sections.title: References to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Employee may refer to employment tribunal if employer fails to provide statement or statement is inaccurate. Tribunal may award 2–4 weeks' pay.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer does not give an employee a statement as required by section 1, 4 or 8, within the time required by the section in question, the employee may require a reference to be made to an employment tribunal to determine what particulars ought to have been included in a statement so as to comply with the requirements of the section in question.

(4) Where on a reference under this section an employment tribunal finds—
(a) that the employer has failed to give the employee any statement required by this Part, or
(b) that a statement given does not comply with the requirements of this Part,
the tribunal shall make a declaration as to what the particulars should be.

sections.title: Right not to suffer unauthorised deductions
sections.summary: Employer must not deduct from wages unless authorised by statute, contract, or prior written consent of worker.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall not make a deduction from wages of a worker employed by him unless—
(a) the deduction is required or authorised to be made by virtue of a statutory provision or a relevant provision of the worker's contract, or
(b) the worker has previously signified in writing his agreement or consent to the making of such a deduction.

(2) In this section "relevant provision", in relation to a worker's contract, means a provision of the contract comprised in one or more written terms of the contract of which the employer has given the worker a copy on an occasion prior to the employer making the deduction in question.

(5) For the purposes of this section an employer makes a deduction from the wages of a worker employed by him if any payment of wages made by the employer to the worker is less than the amount of the wages properly payable by the employer to the worker on that occasion.

sections.title: Excepted deductions
sections.summary: Section 13 does not apply to deductions to recover overpayments, or deductions required by statutory provision.
sections.text:
(1) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer—
(a) to recover an overpayment of wages, or
(b) to recover an overpayment in respect of expenses incurred by the worker in carrying out his employment.

(2) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in consequence of any disciplinary proceedings if those proceedings were held by virtue of a statutory provision.

(3) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in pursuance of a requirement imposed on the employer by a statutory provision to deduct and pay over to a public authority amounts determined by that authority as being due to it from the worker.

sections.title: Complaints to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Worker may complain to employment tribunal within 3 months of deduction that employer has made an unauthorised deduction from wages.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that his employer has made a deduction from his wages in contravention of section 13.

(2) Subject to subsection (4), an employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date of payment of the wages from which the deduction was made.

sections.title: Meaning of "protected disclosure"
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is protected where made by a worker in accordance with ss.43C–43H.
sections.text: In this Act "protected disclosure" means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by section 43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H.
refs: s.103A ERA 1996 (Automatic Unfair Dismissal for Protected Disclosure)

sections.title: Disclosures qualifying for protection
sections.summary: Six categories of wrongdoing qualifying for whistleblower protection. Worker must have reasonable belief the disclosure tends to show the wrongdoing.
sections.text:
(1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of the following—
(a) that a criminal offence has been committed, is being committed or is likely to be committed,
(b) that a person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation to which he is subject,
(c) that a miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur,
(d) that the health or safety of any individual has been, is being or is likely to be endangered,
(e) that the environment has been, is being or is likely to be damaged, or
(f) that information tending to show any matter falling within any one of the preceding paragraphs has been, or is likely to be, deliberately concealed.

(3) A disclosure of information is not a qualifying disclosure if the person making the disclosure commits an offence by making it.

sections.title: Disclosure to prescribed person
sections.summary: Qualifying disclosure to a prescribed regulator (e.g. HSE, FCA, HMRC) is protected provided the worker reasonably believes the relevant failure falls within the regulator's remit.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if—
(a) the worker makes it to a person prescribed by an order made by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section, and
(b) the worker reasonably believes—
(i) that the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which that person is so prescribed, and
(ii) that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true.

sections.title: Protected disclosures — right not to suffer detriment
sections.summary: Worker has right not to be subjected to any detriment by employer on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.

(1A) A worker (W) has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, done by another worker of W's employer in the course of that other worker's employment, on the ground that W has made a protected disclosure.
refs: s.103A ERA 1996 (Automatic Unfair Dismissal for Protected Disclosure)
Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979

sections.title: Right to time off for public duties
sections.summary: Employees who hold certain public positions (magistrates, local councillors, members of statutory tribunals) are entitled to reasonable time off.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall permit an employee of his to take time off during the employee's working hours for the purpose of performing any of the following duties—
(a) any duties as a justice of the peace,
(b) any duties as a member of—
(i) a local authority,
(ii) a statutory tribunal,
(iii) a health authority or NHS foundation trust.

(2) The amount of time off which an employee is to be permitted to take under this section are those that are reasonable in all the circumstances having regard in particular to how much time off is required for the performance of the duties, how much time off the employee has already been permitted, and the circumstances of the employer's business.

sections.title: Right to time off to look for work or arrange training
sections.summary: Employee under notice of redundancy with 2 years' service is entitled to reasonable paid time off to look for new employment or arrange training.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take reasonable time off during the employee's working hours before the end of the notice period in order to—
(a) look for new employment, or
(b) make arrangements for training for future employment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section unless, on whichever is the later of the date on which the notice is given, the employee has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years.
refs: s.53 ERA 1996 (Right to Remuneration for Time Off)

sections.title: Right to time off for ante-natal care
sections.summary: Pregnant employee is entitled to paid time off to keep ante-natal appointments made on advice of a registered medical practitioner, midwife, or nurse.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who—
(a) is pregnant, and
(b) has, on the advice of a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse, made an appointment to attend at any place for the purpose of receiving ante-natal care,
is entitled to be permitted by her employer to take time off during the employee's working hours in order to enable her to keep the appointment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section to keep an appointment unless, if her employer requests her to do so, she produces for her employer's inspection a certificate from a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse stating that the employee is pregnant, and an appointment card showing that the appointment has been made.
refs: Children and Families Act 2014 (Partner Ante-natal Appointments)

sections.title: Time off for dependants
sections.summary: Employee is entitled to reasonable unpaid time off to deal with unexpected problems involving a dependant.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take a reasonable amount of time off during the employee's working hours in order to take action which is necessary—
(a) to provide assistance on an occasion when a dependant falls ill, gives birth or is injured or assaulted,
(b) to make arrangements for the provision of care for a dependant who is ill or injured,
(c) in consequence of the death of a dependant,
(d) because of the unexpected disruption or termination of arrangements for the care of a dependant, or
(e) to deal with an incident which involves a child of the employee and which occurs unexpectedly in a period during which an educational establishment which the child attends is responsible for him.

(2) This section does not apply unless the employee tells his employer the reason for his absence as soon as reasonably practicable.
refs: ACAS Time Off for Dependants Guidance

sections.title: Ordinary maternity leave
sections.summary: Every employee is entitled to 26 weeks' ordinary maternity leave regardless of length of service.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to ordinary maternity leave if she satisfies any conditions which may be prescribed and has complied with any requirements imposed by or under regulations made by the Secretary of State.

(3) Ordinary maternity leave continues for the period of 26 weeks beginning with the day on which it begins, unless the employee is dismissed or the period ends earlier.

sections.title: Additional maternity leave
sections.summary: An employee entitled to ordinary maternity leave is also entitled to 26 weeks' additional maternity leave, giving a total of 52 weeks.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who satisfies prescribed conditions is entitled to additional maternity leave.

(2) Additional maternity leave—
(a) commences on the day after the last day of the employee's ordinary maternity leave period, and
(b) continues for the period of 26 weeks beginning with that day.

sections.title: Redundancy during maternity leave
sections.summary: Where employee on maternity leave is redundant, employer must offer her any suitable available vacancy in preference to other redundant employees.
sections.text:
(1) This section applies where, during an employee's ordinary or additional maternity leave period, it is not practicable by reason of redundancy for the employer to continue to employ her under her existing contract of employment.

(2) Where there is a suitable available vacancy, the employee is entitled to be offered (before the end of her employment under her existing contract) alternative employment with her employer or his successor, or an associated employer, under a new contract of employment which complies with subsection (3).

(3) The new contract of employment must be such that the work to be done under it is of a kind which is both suitable in relation to the employee and appropriate for her to do in the circumstances, and its provisions as to the capacity and place in which she is to be employed are not substantially less favourable to her than if she had continued to be employed under the previous contract.
sections.eraNote: Regulation 29A of the Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999 (as amended) provides that an employee on maternity leave is a priority candidate for any suitable alternative employment during a redundancy exercise. ERA 2025 further strengthens these protections.

sections.title: Parental leave
sections.summary: Employee with one year's service who is the parent of a child under 18 is entitled to 18 weeks' unpaid parental leave in respect of each child.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who has been continuously employed for a period of not less than a year, and has, or expects to have, responsibility for a child, is entitled, in accordance with this section, to be absent from work on parental leave for the purpose of caring for that child.

(2) An employee is entitled to 18 weeks' parental leave in respect of each child in relation to whom he satisfies the conditions in subsection (1)(b).
refs: Children and Families Act 2014 (Shared Parental Leave)
ERA 2025 (Paternity Leave Reform — Not Yet In Force)

sections.title: Right to request flexible working
sections.summary: Any employee may apply to work flexibly from Day 1 of employment. Employer must deal with request in a reasonable manner and may only refuse on one of eight specified business grounds.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying employee may apply to his employer for a change in his terms and conditions of employment if the change relates to—
(i) the hours he is required to work,
(ii) the times when he is required to work,
(iii) where, as between his home and a place of business of his employer, he is required to work, or
(iv) such other aspect of his terms and conditions of employment as the Secretary of State may specify by regulations.
sections.eraNote: From April 2024 (Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023): right to request from Day 1 of employment; employer must consult before refusing; two requests permitted per year. ERA 2025 further strengthens the grounds for refusal and requires employers to consider trial periods.
refs: ACAS Code on Flexible Working Requests 2024

sections.title: Rights of employer and employee to minimum notice
sections.summary: Minimum statutory notice: 1 week per year of service up to 12 weeks after 12+ years. Employee must give 1 week's notice after 1 month's service.
sections.text:
(1) The notice required to be given by an employer to terminate the contract of employment of a person who has been continuously employed for one month or more—
(a) is not less than one week's notice if his period of continuous employment is less than two years,
(b) is not less than one week's notice for each year of continuous employment if his period of continuous employment is two years or more but less than twelve years, and
(c) is not less than twelve weeks' notice if his period of continuous employment is twelve years or more.

(2) The notice required to be given by an employee who has been continuously employed for one month or more to terminate his contract of employment is not less than one week.

sections.title: Right to written statement of reasons for dismissal
sections.summary: Employee with 2 years' service is entitled to a written statement of reasons for dismissal within 14 days of request. Automatically entitled during or at end of pregnancy. ERA 2025 will extend to Day 1.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to be provided by his employer, on request, with a written statement giving particulars of the reasons for the employee's dismissal.

(2) An employee is entitled to make a request under this section only if he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(4) An employee is not required to have been continuously employed for any period to make a request under this section if she is dismissed at any time while she is pregnant, or after childbirth in circumstances in which her ordinary or additional maternity leave period ends by reason of the dismissal.
sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 s.4 will remove the 2-year qualifying period, making written reasons a Day 1 right for all employees. Not yet in force.
refs: ERA 2025 s.4 (Written Reasons — Day 1 Right — Not Yet In Force)

sections.title: The right
sections.summary: Every employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed.
sections.text:
(1) An employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed by his employer.

(2) Subsection (1) has effect subject to the following provisions of this Part (in particular sections 108 to 110) and to the provisions of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992.

sections.title: Circumstances in which an employee is dismissed
sections.summary: Dismissal occurs when employer terminates contract, limited-term contract expires without renewal, or employee is entitled to and does terminate in response to employer conduct (constructive dismissal).
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Part an employee is dismissed by his employer if (and, subject to subsection (2) and section 96, only if)—
(a) the contract under which he is employed is terminated by the employer (whether with or without notice),
(b) he is employed under a limited-term contract and that contract terminates by virtue of the limiting event without being renewed under the same contract, or
(c) the employee terminates the contract under which he is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in which he is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the employer's conduct.
refs: Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp [1978] (Constructive Dismissal)

sections.title: General — potentially fair reasons and reasonableness
sections.summary: Employer must show a potentially fair reason for dismissal (capability, conduct, redundancy, illegality, SOSR) then tribunal determines whether dismissal was reasonable.
sections.text:
(1) In determining for the purposes of this Part whether the dismissal of an employee is fair or unfair, it is for the employer to show—
(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal, and
(b) that it is either a reason falling within subsection (2) or some other substantial reason of a kind such as to justify the dismissal of an employee holding the position which the employee held.

(2) A reason falls within this subsection if it—
(a) relates to the capability or qualifications of the employee for performing work of the kind which he was employed by the employer to do,
(b) relates to the conduct of the employee,
(c) is that the employee was redundant, or
(d) is that the employee could not continue to work in the position which he held without contravention of a duty or restriction imposed by or under an enactment.

(4) In any other case where the employer has fulfilled the requirements of subsection (1), the determination of the question whether the dismissal is fair or unfair depends on whether in the circumstances (including the size and administrative resources of the employer's undertaking) the employer acted reasonably or unreasonably in treating it as a sufficient reason for dismissing the employee, and shall be determined in accordance with equity and the substantial merits of the case.
refs: British Home Stores v Burchell [1980] ICR 303 (Conduct — Belief Test)
Iceland Frozen Foods v Jones [1983] ICR 17 (Band of Reasonable Responses)
ACAS Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures 2015

sections.title: Leave for family reasons — automatic unfair dismissal
sections.summary: Dismissal for reasons connected with pregnancy, maternity, paternity, adoption, parental leave, or shared parental leave is automatically unfair regardless of length of service.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if the reason or principal reason for the dismissal is of a prescribed kind.

(3) A reason or set of circumstances prescribed under this section must relate to—
(a) pregnancy, childbirth or maternity,
(b) ordinary, compulsory or additional maternity leave,
(c) ordinary or additional adoption leave,
(ca) shared parental leave,
(d) parental leave,
(e) paternity leave.

(4) Dismissal in circumstances prescribed under this section is to be regarded as automatically unfair regardless of any qualifying period.

sections.title: Health and safety cases — automatic unfair dismissal
sections.summary: Dismissal for health and safety reasons including designating H&S representatives, raising H&S concerns, or leaving a place of danger is automatically unfair.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that—
(a) having been designated by the employer to carry out activities in connection with preventing or reducing risks to health and safety at work, the employee carried out (or proposed to carry out) any such activities,
(b) being a representative of workers on matters of health and safety at work or member of a safety committee, the employee performed (or proposed to perform) any functions as such a representative or a member of such a committee,
(c) being an employee at a place where there was no such representative or safety committee, or it was not reasonably practicable to raise the matter by those means, the employee brought to his employer's attention, by reasonable means, circumstances connected with his work which he reasonably believed were harmful or potentially harmful to health or safety.
refs: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999

sections.title: Protected disclosure — automatic unfair dismissal
sections.summary: Dismissal for making a protected disclosure is automatically unfair regardless of length of service. Compensation is uncapped.
sections.text: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure.

sections.title: Qualifying period of employment
sections.summary: Currently requires two years' continuous employment before an employee can claim ordinary unfair dismissal under s.94. The qualifying period does not apply to automatically unfair dismissals (s.108(3)). ERA 2025 Schedule 2 will repeal s.108 entirely when commenced, creating day-one unfair dismissal rights — the most significant change to unfair dismissal law since the right was introduced in 1971.
sections.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if—

(aa) section 98ZA applies,

(b) section 99 (read with any regulations under that section) applies,

(c) subsection (1) of section 100 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(d) subsection (1) of section 101 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(da) section 101B applies,

(dd) section 104A applies,

(de) section 104B applies,

(df) section 104C applies,

(dg) section 104D applies,

(e) section 102 applies,

(f) section 103 applies,

(ff) section 103A applies,

(g) subsection (1) of section 104 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(gg) section 104E applies,

(gh) section 104F applies,

(gi) section 104G applies,

(gj) section 104H applies,

(gk) section 104I applies,

(gl) section 104J applies,

(gm) section 104K applies,

(h) section 105 applies,

(hh) paragraph 5(3) of the Schedule to the Reserved Forces (Safeguard of Employment) Act 1985 (c.17) applies,

(i) section 23A or 23B of the Employment Relations Act 1999 applies.
sections.keyPrinciple: The qualifying period is a gateway condition, not a merits test. Satisfying it merely opens the door to s.94; it does not determine outcome. The period has fluctuated between 6 months and 2 years since the right to claim unfair dismissal was created by the Industrial Relations Act 1971. ERA 2025 will repeal s.108 entirely — the first time in 54 years that there will be no qualifying period at all.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if—

(a) section 84 or 96(1) applies,

(b) subsection (1) of section 99 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(c) subsection (1) of section 100 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(d) subsection (1) of section 101 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(e) section 102 applies,

(f) section 103 applies,

(g) subsection (1) of section 104 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies, or

(h) section 105 applies.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978 s.64)
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the two-year qualifying period that had been in place since the Employment Act 1985 s.6 increased it from one year. The Conservative Government's position was that a longer qualifying period encouraged employers to hire by reducing the risk of unfair dismissal claims in the early period of employment. The original s.108(3) listed 8 exceptions (automatically unfair reasons) that bypassed the qualifying period.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than one year ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "one year" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if— [list of exceptions, expanding through this period as new automatically unfair reasons were enacted]
versions.amendedBy: Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1999 (SI 1999/1436)
versions.policyRationale: The Blair Government reduced the qualifying period from two years to one year as part of the Fairness at Work programme. The White Paper (Cm 3968) originally proposed a reduction to six months, but one year was the compromise reached after consultation with employer representatives. The reduction was implemented by statutory instrument under ERA 1996 s.236(3), not by primary legislation — demonstrating that the qualifying period can be changed without an Act of Parliament. The TUC argued for complete abolition; the CBI argued for retention at two years.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if— [current list of 20+ exceptions — see full text above]
versions.amendedBy: Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989)
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government restored the two-year qualifying period as part of the Employment Law Review announced in the Growth Review 2011. The stated aim was to reduce the regulatory burden on employers and encourage hiring by removing the risk of unfair dismissal claims during the first two years of employment. The BIS Impact Assessment estimated the change would result in an additional 2,000-3,000 employees per year losing access to the unfair dismissal jurisdiction. The transitional provision (SI 2012/989 art.4) preserved the one-year period for employees whose continuous employment began before 6 April 2012.

pendingAmendment.effect: Repeals section 108 entirely. Does not substitute a shorter period — it removes the qualifying period concept altogether. Every employee will have the right to claim unfair dismissal from day one of employment. However, ERA 2025 introduces an 'initial period of employment' (a statutory probation period, expected to be around 9 months) during which a simplified 'lighter touch' dismissal process will apply. Compensation for unfair dismissal during the initial period is expected to be lower than for established employees.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026. ERA 2025 received Royal Assent but the s.108 repeal has not been commenced. The Government's stated intention is Autumn 2026 at the earliest, potentially applying only to new joiners from the commencement date.
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: Autumn 2026 at the earliest — subject to consultation outcomes on the initial period of employment
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: CRITICAL — affects every employer in Great Britain. Every dismissal from day one of employment will carry unfair dismissal exposure once commenced. Employers who currently rely on the two-year qualifying period to manage out short-service employees without formal procedures will face a fundamentally different risk landscape. The ACEI unfair dismissal category will require recalibration to reflect universal day-one exposure.

initialPeriodOfEmployment.concept: ERA 2025 introduces a new concept of an 'initial period of employment' — effectively a statutory probation period — during which the employer may dismiss using a 'lighter touch' process. The period is expected to be around 9 months but the exact duration and the procedural requirements are subject to consultation.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.lighterTouchProcess: The Government has indicated that employers will need to: (1) hold a meeting with the employee to discuss concerns; (2) allow the employee to be accompanied by a trade union representative or colleague; (3) make a decision on dismissal. This is simpler than the full ACAS Code procedure but still requires a structured process — dismissal without any process will remain unfair.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.compensationDuringIPE: Compensation for unfair dismissal during the initial period is expected to be lower than the standard compensatory award, but the exact quantum is subject to consultation.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.consultationStatus: Consultation ongoing as at March 2026. Final regulations not yet laid.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 Schedule 2 repeals s.108 entirely. This is not a reduction to zero — it is a repeal. Section 108 will cease to exist. A future government wishing to reintroduce a qualifying period would need primary legislation, not a statutory instrument. This makes the change structurally permanent in a way that previous qualifying period changes (which were done by SI) were not.

pepperVHartStatements.statement: The reforms are not about making it easier to dismiss workers or to undermine job security. Day one rights are about ensuring that from the first day in a new job, workers have the security of knowing they cannot be unfairly dismissed. The initial period of employment will give employers confidence that they can assess whether a new recruit is right for the role.
pepperVHartStatements.speaker: Jonathan Reynolds MP, Secretary of State for Business and Trade
pepperVHartStatements.significance: The ministerial statement at Second Reading of the Employment Rights Bill (now ERA 2025) addressing the purpose and scope of the qualifying period removal. Admissible under Pepper v Hart [1993] UKHL 3 to establish parliamentary intent regarding the initial period of employment concept.

precedingLegislation.notes: The right to claim unfair dismissal was created for the first time. Qualifying period set at 26 weeks.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Trade Union and Labour Relations Act 1974 Sch.1 para.4
precedingLegislation.notes: The 1974 Act retained the 26-week qualifying period.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Unfair Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1979 (SI 1979/959)
precedingLegislation.notes: Callaghan Government increased the qualifying period to 1 year.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Unfair Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1985 (SI 1985/782)
precedingLegislation.notes: Thatcher Government increased the qualifying period to 2 years for employers with fewer than 20 employees.
Extended the 2-year qualifying period to all employers regardless of size.
Consolidated the existing 2-year qualifying period from EPCA 1978.
Blair Government reduced to 1 year under Fairness at Work programme.
Coalition Government restored 2 years under Growth Review.
Starmer Government — complete repeal. First time in 54 years with no qualifying period.

refs: ERA 1996 s.94 (the right not to be unfairly dismissed — s.108 is the gateway condition for s.94)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (exceptions — automatically unfair reasons that bypass the qualifying period: over 20 categories)
ERA 1996 s.236(3) (power to vary qualifying period by statutory instrument — used in 1999 and 2012)
ERA 1996 s.211 (definition of continuous employment — starts from the beginning of employment, not from date of contract)
ERA 1996 s.212 (weeks counting in computing period of continuous employment)
ERA 1996 s.64(2) (health requirements — one month qualifying period under s.108(2))
ERA 2025 Schedule 2 (repeal of s.108 — not yet in force)
Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1999 (SI 1999/1436)
Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989)
Fairness at Work White Paper (Cm 3968, 1998) — proposed 6 months, compromised at 1 year
BIS Growth Review 2011 — rationale for restoration to 2 years
House of Commons Library Research Briefing CBP-8347 (Unfair dismissal: qualifying service rule)
R v Secretary of State for Employment, ex parte Seymour-Smith [2000] UKHL 12
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)

leadingCases.name: R v Secretary of State for Employment, ex parte Seymour-Smith and Perez
leadingCases.principle: The two-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal was indirectly discriminatory against women (because a higher proportion of women than men had less than two years' continuous service), but was justified as a proportionate means of achieving the legitimate aim of encouraging recruitment.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords, following a preliminary ruling from the CJEU (C-167/97), held that the qualifying period had a disparate adverse impact on women. However, by a 3-2 majority, the Lords held that the Government had discharged the burden of showing that the qualifying period was justified as encouraging employers to recruit by reducing the perceived risk of unfair dismissal claims. Lord Nicholls dissented, arguing the Government had not produced sufficient evidence of a causal link between the qualifying period and increased recruitment.
leadingCases.significance: The leading UKHL authority on the legality of the qualifying period. Although the challenge ultimately failed, the case established that the qualifying period IS indirectly discriminatory against women and can only be maintained if objectively justified. This has implications for the ERA 2025 initial period of employment — any new qualifying threshold will need to pass the same justification test. The dissenting judgment of Lord Nicholls on evidential burden is frequently cited in discrimination scholarship.

leadingCases.name: Secretary of State for Justice v Slee
leadingCases.principle: The qualifying period runs from the beginning of the employee's employment, not from the date they entered their current role or position. Continuous employment across different roles with the same employer counts.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that continuous employment for the purposes of s.108 is calculated from the date the employee began employment with the employer (or an associated employer), regardless of changes in role, location, or terms during that period. The employee does not need to have been in the same role for the qualifying period.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the basic but frequently misunderstood principle that the qualifying period relates to continuous employment with the employer, not continuous service in a particular role. Important for employers who restructure or redeploy during the qualifying period.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Checking if your employee can make an unfair dismissal claim
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the qualifying period, including the exceptions for automatically unfair dismissals and the calculation of continuous employment. Confirms that the two-year qualifying period is the current position but notes the ERA 2025 changes.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Unfair dismissal: qualifying service rule (CBP-8347)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive parliamentary briefing on the history of the qualifying period from 1971 to 2025, including the full legislative chronology, the Seymour-Smith challenge, and the ERA 2025 repeal. Updated to reflect current position.

commonErrors: Assuming the qualifying period protects employers from ALL unfair dismissal claims — s.108(3) lists over 20 categories of automatically unfair dismissal that bypass the qualifying period entirely. Pregnancy, whistleblowing, health and safety, trade union activities, assertion of statutory rights, and many others are day-one rights NOW, regardless of s.108(1)

Calculating continuous employment from the date of the current contract rather than from the date employment began — if an employee has been employed by the same employer (or an associated employer) in different roles, the continuous employment runs from the original start date

Not recognising the transitional provisions — the 2012 increase from 1 year to 2 years only applies to employees whose continuous employment began on or after 6 April 2012. Employees who started before that date retain the 1-year qualifying period. Similarly, when ERA 2025 commences, transitional provisions may apply

Treating the qualifying period as a 'safe harbour' during which the employer can dismiss for any reason — even during the qualifying period, dismissals can still be challenged as automatically unfair (s.108(3)), discriminatory (EqA 2010), wrongful (breach of contract), or in breach of other statutory rights

Not preparing for the ERA 2025 repeal — s.108 will be repealed entirely, not amended. A future government cannot simply issue a statutory instrument to reinstate a qualifying period (as was done in 1999 and 2012) because there will be no s.108 to vary. Primary legislation would be required. This makes the change structurally permanent

Overlooking the interaction between the qualifying period and the effective date of termination — the qualifying period must be satisfied 'ending with the effective date of termination' (s.108(1)). Where the employer gives notice, the effective date may be the end of the notice period, not the date notice was given, potentially extending the qualifying period calculation in the employee's favour

Misunderstanding the 'initial period of employment' concept in ERA 2025 — this is NOT a new qualifying period. It is a statutory probation period during which a 'lighter touch' dismissal process applies. The employee will still have the right to claim unfair dismissal during the initial period; it is the procedural requirements and compensation that differ, not the existence of the right

Not recognising that the qualifying period does not apply to redundancy payment claims — ERA 1996 s.155 sets a separate two-year qualifying period for statutory redundancy payments, which is NOT affected by the ERA 2025 repeal of s.108. The redundancy payment qualifying period remains at two years

sections.title: Complaints to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Unfair dismissal claim must be presented within 3 months of effective date of termination (subject to ACAS early conciliation extension).
sections.text:
(1) A complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal against an employer by any person that he was unfairly dismissed by the employer.

(2) Subject to the following provisions of this section, an employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with the effective date of termination.

sections.title: Basic award
sections.summary: Basic award calculated by reference to age, length of service (up to 20 years), and week's pay (subject to statutory cap). Same formula as statutory redundancy payment.
sections.text:
(1) The amount of the basic award shall be calculated by—
(a) determining the period, ending with the effective date of termination, during which the employee has been continuously employed,
(b) reckoning backwards from the end of that period the number of years of employment falling within that period, and
(c) allowing the appropriate amount for each of those years of employment.

(2) In subsection (1)(c) "the appropriate amount" means—
(a) one and a half weeks' pay for a year of employment in which the employee was not below the age of 41,
(b) one week's pay for a year of employment (not within paragraph (a)) in which the employee was not below the age of 22, and
(c) half a week's pay for each such year of employment not within paragraph (a) or (b).

(3) Where twenty years of employment have been reckoned under subsection (1), no account shall be taken of any period of employment earlier than those twenty years.
refs: Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order (annual uprating)

sections.title: Compensatory award
sections.summary: Compensatory award is such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable having regard to loss suffered. Subject to statutory cap (lower of 52 weeks' pay or the annual limit).
sections.text:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this section and sections 124, 124A and 126, the amount of the compensatory award shall be such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances having regard to the loss sustained by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal in so far as that loss is attributable to action taken by the employer.

(2) The loss referred to in subsection (1) shall be taken to include any expenses reasonably incurred by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal, and loss of any benefit which he might reasonably be expected to have had but for the dismissal.

(4) In ascertaining the loss referred to in subsection (1) the tribunal shall apply the same rule concerning the duty of a person to mitigate his loss as applies to damages recoverable under the common law of England and Wales or Scotland.
refs: Polkey v AE Dayton Services [1988] (Procedural Defects)
Norton Tool Co Ltd v Tewson [1973] (Mitigation)

sections.title: Limit of compensatory award
sections.summary: Compensatory award capped at lower of 52 weeks' gross pay or £115,115 (April 2024, reviewed annually). No cap for protected disclosure or health and safety dismissals.
sections.text:
(1) The amount of any compensatory award shall not exceed the amount specified in subsection (1ZA).

(1ZA) The amount specified in this subsection is the lower of—
(a) £115,115, and
(b) 52 multiplied by a week's pay of the person.

(1A) The reference in subsection (1) to the specified amount does not apply where the dismissal is a protected disclosure dismissal (s.103A) or a health and safety dismissal (s.100(1)(a) and (b)).
refs: Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order (annual uprating)

versions.text: The compensatory award was subject to a statutory cap of £11,300 (consolidated from EPCA 1978 as uprated).
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as originally enacted (with annual uprating orders)
versions.policyRationale: Consolidated the compensation cap regime from EPCA 1978. The cap was subject to annual uprating by statutory instrument.

versions.text: The compensatory award was subject to a statutory cap of £50,000 (raised by ERA 1999), subsequently uprated annually.
versions.policyRationale: The ERA 1999 Explanatory Notes stated that the previous £12,000 limit meant individuals could not be fully compensated for their loss. The government raised it to £50,000 (having originally considered abolishing the cap entirely but retreated following consultation).

versions.text: (1ZA) The amount specified in this subsection is the lower of— (a) £74,200 [initially; uprated annually], and (b) 52 multiplied by a week's pay of the person.
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.15
versions.policyRationale: The ERRA 2013 introduced the 'lower of' formula — capping awards at the lower of a fixed amount (uprated annually) or 52 weeks' gross pay. This addressed concerns that high earners could receive disproportionate awards. The fixed amount reached £115,115 by April 2024.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 contains provision to remove the statutory cap on compensatory awards for ordinary unfair dismissal. However, this provision is subject to a review or impact assessment before commencement, following Lords amendments during the Bill's passage.
pendingAmendment.anticipatedCommencement: Subject to review — no confirmed date
pendingAmendment.policyRationale: The government proposed removing the cap to ensure full compensation for unfairly dismissed employees, but accepted Lords amendments requiring further assessment before implementation.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: CRITICAL — removal of the cap would transform employer exposure calculations across all unfair dismissal categories.

sections.keyPrinciple: The compensatory award cap limits the maximum compensation for unfair dismissal. It does not apply to protected disclosure (s.103A) or health and safety (s.100) dismissals, which are uncapped.

sections.title: The right
sections.summary: Employee dismissed by reason of redundancy with 2 years' qualifying service is entitled to a statutory redundancy payment.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall pay a redundancy payment to any employee of his if the employee—
(a) is dismissed by the employer by reason of redundancy, or
(b) is eligible for a redundancy payment by reason of being laid off or kept on short-time.

sections.title: Redundancy
sections.summary: Redundancy occurs where employer ceases or intends to cease carrying on business, or where requirements for work of a particular kind have ceased or diminished.
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Act an employee who is dismissed shall be taken to be dismissed by reason of redundancy if the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to—
(a) the fact that his employer has ceased or intends to cease to carry on the business for the purposes of which the employee was employed by him, or to carry on that business in the place where the employee was employed, or
(b) the fact that the requirements of that business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind, or for employees to carry out work of a particular kind in the place where the employee was employed by the employer, have ceased or diminished or are expected to cease or diminish.

(6) "Cease or diminish" means cease or diminish either permanently or temporarily and for whatever reason.
refs: Safeway Stores plc v Burrell [1997] (Function v Job)

sections.title: Qualifying period of employment
sections.summary: Employee must have 2 years' continuous employment to be entitled to a redundancy payment.
sections.text: An employee does not have any right to a redundancy payment unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the relevant date.

sections.title: Amount of redundancy payment
sections.summary: Statutory redundancy payment calculated by reference to age, years of service (up to 20), and week's pay (capped). Same formula as basic award for unfair dismissal.
sections.text:
(1) The amount of a redundancy payment shall be calculated by—
(a) determining the period, ending with the relevant date, during which the employee has been continuously employed,
(b) reckoning backwards from the end of that period the number of years of employment falling within that period, and
(c) allowing the appropriate amount for each of those years of employment.

(2) In subsection (1)(c) "the appropriate amount" means—
(a) one and a half weeks' pay for a year of employment in which the employee was not below the age of 41,
(b) one week's pay for a year of employment (not within paragraph (a)) in which the employee was not below the age of 22, and
(c) half a week's pay for each such year of employment not within paragraph (a) or (b).

(3) Where twenty years of employment have been reckoned under subsection (1), no account shall be taken under that subsection of any period of employment earlier than those twenty years.
refs: s.227 ERA 1996 (Maximum Week's Pay — reviewed annually)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order (annual uprating)

sections.title: Continuity of employment
sections.summary: Provisions governing how continuous employment is computed. Critical for qualifying periods, notice, redundancy, and unfair dismissal.
sections.text:
(1) References in any provision of this Act to a period of continuous employment are to a period computed in accordance with this Chapter.

(2) In any provision of this Act which refers to a period of continuous employment expressed in months or years—
(a) a month means a calendar month, and
(b) a year means a year of twelve calendar months.

(3) In computing an employee's period of continuous employment for the purposes of any provision of this Act, any question whether the employee's employment is of a kind counting towards a period of continuous employment shall be determined week by week.

sections.title: Weeks counting in computing period
sections.summary: A week counts towards continuous employment if the employee's relations with the employer are governed by a contract of employment throughout the week.
sections.text:
(1) Any week during the whole or part of which an employee's relations with his employer are governed by a contract of employment counts in computing the employee's period of continuous employment.

(3) Subject to subsection (4), any week during the whole or part of which an employee is—
(a) incapable of work in consequence of sickness or injury,
(b) absent from work on account of a temporary cessation of work, or
(c) absent from work in circumstances such that, by arrangement or custom, he is regarded as continuing in the employment of his employer for any purpose,
counts in computing the employee's period of continuous employment.
refs: Ford v Warwickshire County Council [1983] HL (Temporary Cessation)

sections.title: Maximum amount of a week's pay
sections.summary: The maximum weekly pay used for statutory calculations is subject to a statutory cap revised annually by order. £643 per week from April 2024.
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Chapter the amount of a week's pay of an employee shall not exceed the amount specified by order of the Secretary of State, currently £643 per week (from April 2024, reviewed annually).

(2) The Secretary of State may by order increase the amount mentioned in subsection (1).
refs: Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order (annual uprating)

sections.title: "Employee", "employer", "worker" etc
sections.summary: Defines employee as an individual working under a contract of employment. Worker has wider meaning including some self-employed individuals.
sections.text:
(1) In this Act "employee" means an individual who has entered into or works under (or, where the employment has ceased, worked under) a contract of employment.

(2) In this Act "contract of employment" means a contract of service or apprenticeship, whether express or implied, and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing.

(3) In this Act "worker" means an individual who has entered into or works under—
(a) a contract of employment, or
(b) any other contract, whether express or implied and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing, whereby the individual undertakes to do or perform personally any work or services for another party to the contract whose status is not by virtue of the contract that of a client or customer of any profession or business undertaking carried on by the individual.
refs: Ready Mixed Concrete (South East) Ltd v Minister of Pensions [1968] (Employment Status Tests)
Autoclenz Ltd v Belcher [2011] UKSC 41 (Sham Clauses)
Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5 (Worker Status)

readings.keyPoints: Consolidation Bill introduced in the House of Lords. As a consolidation measure, the Bill was presented with minimal debate and referred to the Joint Committee on Consolidation Bills in accordance with standard parliamentary procedure for consolidation measures.
readings.significance: Consolidation Bills are introduced in the Lords and referred to the Joint Committee. They receive minimal floor debate because the Joint Committee scrutinises on behalf of Parliament.

readings.hansardRef: Joint Committee on Consolidation Bills, Session 1995-96
readings.keyPoints: The Joint Committee examined the Bill to ensure it accurately consolidated existing legislation without making substantive changes to the law. The Committee comprises members of both Houses and scrutinises consolidation proposals on behalf of Parliament.
readings.significance: The Joint Committee's approval is the primary quality assurance mechanism for consolidation Bills. Its report confirmed the Bill properly consolidated the existing law.

readings.keyPoints: Bill passed the House of Lords following the Joint Committee's report.
readings.significance: Standard procedure for consolidation Bills — passed without division following Joint Committee scrutiny.

readings.keyPoints: Consolidation Bill received Second Reading in the House of Commons. As with all consolidation measures, debate was formal and procedural.
readings.significance: The Commons relies on the Joint Committee's scrutiny and does not re-examine the substance of consolidation Bills.

readings.keyPoints: Employment Rights Act 1996 (c.18) received Royal Assent on 22 May 1996, alongside the Employment Tribunals Act 1996 (c.17) and the Industrial Tribunals Act 1996 (c.17).
readings.significance: The ERA 1996, ETA 1996, and related consolidation Acts came into force together, replacing the EPCA 1978 and related legislation. This was the largest consolidation of employment law since 1978.

committeeStage.note: Consolidation Bills do not receive substantive amendments. The Joint Committee's role is to verify that the Bill accurately consolidates existing law without making changes to it. Any corrections recommended by the Law Commission for clarity are incorporated by Parliamentary Counsel.

keyDebates.ministerialStatement: The Lord Chancellor presented the Employment Rights Bill as a consolidation of the Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978 and related enactments, drafted by Parliamentary Counsel at the Law Commission.
keyDebates.significance: Established that the ERA 1996 was purely consolidating and did not make substantive changes to the law.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978
precedingDocuments.significance: The primary predecessor statute. Most of ERA 1996 re-enacts EPCA 1978 provisions. EPCA 1978 itself consolidated earlier legislation including the Contracts of Employment Act 1963, Redundancy Payments Act 1965, and Employment Protection Act 1975.

precedingDocuments.title: Wages Act 1986
precedingDocuments.significance: Protection of wages provisions (now Part II ERA 1996 ss.13-27) were consolidated from this Act.

precedingDocuments.title: Trade Union Reform and Employment Rights Act 1993
precedingDocuments.significance: Made significant amendments to EPCA 1978 before consolidation, including maternity provisions and employment tribunal procedures. These amendments were incorporated into ERA 1996.

precedingDocuments.title: Law Commission Report on Consolidation
precedingDocuments.significance: The Law Commission prepared the consolidation under its programme of statute law reform. Parliamentary Counsel drafted the Bill under the Commission's direction.

explanatoryNotes.note: The Explanatory Notes for ERA 1996 are limited in scope, as is typical for consolidation Acts. They confirm that the Act consolidates the Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978 and related legislation. Explanatory Notes were not routinely published for Acts until the 1998-99 parliamentary session; the ERA 1996 notes were added retrospectively.

leadingCases.name: British Home Stores Ltd v Burchell [1980] ICR 303
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established the three-stage test for misconduct dismissals: (1) the employer genuinely believed the employee was guilty of misconduct; (2) the employer had reasonable grounds for that belief; (3) at the time the belief was formed, the employer had carried out as much investigation as was reasonable in the circumstances.
leadingCases.significance: The Burchell test remains the foundational framework for assessing fairness in conduct dismissals under s.98 ERA 1996. Applied in virtually every misconduct unfair dismissal case.

leadingCases.name: Iceland Frozen Foods Ltd v Jones [1983] ICR 17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the function of the tribunal is not to substitute its own view for that of the employer, but to determine whether the employer's decision fell within the range of reasonable responses open to a reasonable employer.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'band of reasonable responses' test for s.98(4) reasonableness, which remains the governing standard for all unfair dismissal claims.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1988] ICR 142
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that procedural defects in a dismissal process render the dismissal unfair, even if the employer would have dismissed fairly had proper procedures been followed. However, the tribunal may reduce the compensatory award to reflect the chance that dismissal would have occurred in any event.
leadingCases.significance: The Polkey principle governs the treatment of procedural unfairness in unfair dismissal cases. The 'Polkey reduction' is a standard feature of compensatory award calculations.

leadingCases.name: Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp [1978] QB 761
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that constructive dismissal under s.95(1)(c) requires the employer to have committed a repudiatory breach of contract — the contractual test applies, not a test of unreasonableness.
leadingCases.significance: Established the contractual basis for constructive dismissal claims. An employee must show a fundamental breach of contract by the employer to establish constructive dismissal.

leadingCases.name: Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held unanimously that Uber drivers were 'workers' within the meaning of s.230(3) ERA 1996. The contractual terms could not determine the question — the tribunal must look at the reality of the relationship.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on worker status under s.230 ERA 1996. Confirmed that courts must examine the reality of the working relationship, not merely the written contractual terms.

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a disclosure can be in the 'public interest' for the purposes of s.43B(1) even where the worker has a personal interest in the disclosure. The tribunal should consider the numbers affected, the nature of the interests, the nature of the wrongdoing, and the identity of the wrongdoer.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the 'public interest' requirement for protected disclosures under s.43B(1) ERA 1996, as amended by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013.

leadingCases.name: Autoclenz Ltd v Belcher [2011] UKSC 41
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that employment tribunals should look beyond the written terms of a contract to determine the true nature of the relationship. Sham or artificial contractual terms designed to deny worker status are to be disregarded.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the purposive approach to employment status determination under s.230 ERA 1996. Tribunals must examine the reality, not just the paperwork.

leadingCases.name: Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd [1999] UKHL 44
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the statutory definition of redundancy in s.139 ERA 1996 requires only that the dismissal is attributable to a diminution in the requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind. There is no need to identify the specific employees whose work has diminished.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive authority on the statutory definition of redundancy. Resolved the 'function test' vs 'contract test' debate in favour of a straightforward statutory construction.
